section_3_reasons_for_protest.other_reason_for_protest: The primary basis for my protest is that the current year’s appraised value does not reasonably reflect the property’s true market value given its actual physical condition, needed repairs, and recent comparable sales, and that the characteristics used in the appraisal records (square footage, condition rating, and certain improvement details) are partially inaccurate, which appears to have contributed to an inflated value.

section_5_statement_of_facts_or_argument.statement_of_facts_or_arguments_line_1: I, Michael Andrew Ramirez, am the owner and occupant of the single‑family residence located at 4821 Lost Creek Bend, Austin, TX 78746, within the Travis Central Appraisal District. I am protesting the 2025 appraised (market) value of my property because it was increased from $875,000 in 2024 to $1,045,000 in 2025 (a 19.4% increase), which I believe overstates the true market value given the home’s age (built in 1982), deferred maintenance issues, and recent verified sales of similar nearby homes that are in significantly better condition than mine. I purchased this home in 2016 and have lived here continuously with my spouse and two children, so I am very familiar with its physical condition, needed repairs, and limitations compared to renovated properties in the same subdivision.
section_5_statement_of_facts_or_argument.statement_of_facts_or_arguments_line_2: The appraisal district’s online record currently lists my property as 2,610 square feet of living area, in “Good” condition, with an updated kitchen and partially updated bathrooms. Based on two separate measurements—one done by my real estate agent in 2021 using a laser measure and one that I completed myself in March 2025 following ANSI measurement guidelines—the actual air‑conditioned living area is approximately 2,470 square feet, about 140 square feet less than what the district has on file. I have attached a floor plan sketch and the agent’s measurement summary as evidence. In addition, the home has not had any major interior renovations since approximately 2005 (prior owner). The kitchen cabinets are original to that remodel and are now worn, several appliances are at or near the end of their useful life, most of the flooring is original 1980s tile or scratched engineered wood, and both upstairs bathrooms have original tub surrounds, dated fixtures, and visible grout deterioration. I have included labeled photos showing the worn kitchen countertops, water‑stained drywall in the master bathroom ceiling from a prior roof leak, and cracking in the living‑room ceiling near a settlement line. These condition issues mean my property is closer to “Average” or even “Below Average” compared to the fully remodeled homes the district appears to be using as primary comparables.
section_5_statement_of_facts_or_argument.statement_of_facts_or_arguments_line_3: To support my opinion of value, I reviewed 2024–early 2025 closed sales within roughly a half‑mile radius in the same school zone. I obtained MLS printouts for four nearby properties: (1) 4809 Lost Creek Bend, a fully renovated 2,650 sq. ft. home with modern kitchen, new roof, and updated baths, which sold in October 2024 for $995,000; (2) 1204 Lost Creek Circle, a 2,540 sq. ft. home with extensive 2020 renovations, which sold in December 2024 for $1,020,000; (3) 4703 Lost Creek Cove, a 2,430 sq. ft. home in noticeably better cosmetic condition than mine but not fully updated, which sold for $930,000 in August 2024; and (4) 5003 Bendridge Trail, a slightly larger 2,720 sq. ft. home with a pool and updated interior that sold for $1,060,000 in January 2025. All four of these properties have either substantially updated interiors, additional amenities (such as a pool), or larger square footage than my home. After adjusting for condition, size, and amenities using the agent’s informal estimate (I have attached her email and adjustment worksheet), a reasonable indicated value for my property as of January 1, 2025, is in the range of $900,000–$925,000. I am requesting that the ARB reduce my appraised value to $915,000, which I believe fairly reflects what a typical buyer would pay for my home in its current, unrenovated condition and corrects the overstatement caused by the inflated square footage and “Good” condition rating currently shown in the appraisal district’s records.
